papers.title: Real-Time AI Service Economy: A Framework for Agentic Computing Across the Continuum
papers.innovation: Proposes a comprehensive framework for orchestrating and managing autonomous AI agents and their latency-sensitive workloads across the entire device-edge-cloud continuum. It addresses challenges related to multi-stage processing, resource competition, policy, and governance constraints in real-time, distributed AI systems.
papers.benchmarks: This paper establishes an architectural framework rather than proposing specific datasets. An AI Engineer would use its principles to design systems that meet stringent real-time performance metrics (e.g., latency, throughput) and resource utilization benchmarks under varying operational policies and infrastructure loads.
papers.use_case: Designing, deploying, and managing scalable and robust real-time AI agent services in complex distributed environments. This is crucial for applications such as autonomous systems, smart cities, industrial automation, or large-scale intelligent infrastructure where agents operate across heterogeneous compute resources.

papers.title: The World Won't Stay Still: Programmable Evolution for Agent Benchmarks
papers.innovation: Introduces a novel approach for developing benchmarks that incorporate 'programmable evolution' to simulate dynamic real-world environments. This allows for rigorous evaluation of LLM-powered agents' robustness and adaptability to changes in environments, schemas, and toolsets, moving beyond static testing scenarios.
papers.benchmarks: The paper itself is focused on advancing benchmark methodologies. It aims to create more realistic and challenging benchmarks by allowing environments and their components to evolve, thereby providing a robust measure of an agent's ability to cope with unexpected changes and maintain performance over time.
papers.use_case: Developing and evaluating highly robust and adaptable LLM-powered agents for real-world deployment. AI engineers can leverage these evolving benchmarks to stress-test their agents against changes in API specifications, data structures, environmental rules, or available tools, ensuring long-term reliability and resilience.

papers.title: Agentic LLM Planning via Step-Wise PDDL Simulation: An Empirical Characterisation
papers.innovation: Provides an empirical characterization of Large Language Models' (LLMs) capabilities in task planning, specifically by evaluating their performance against classical symbolic planning methods using PDDL (Planning Domain Definition Language) simulation. It sheds light on the viability and limitations of LLMs as planners for autonomous systems.
papers.benchmarks: The 'empirical characterisation' serves as a benchmark for LLM planning abilities. It likely employs standard planning domains and scenarios expressed in PDDL to compare LLM-generated plans' correctness, optimality, and efficiency against traditional AI planning algorithms, offering a practical assessment for engineers.
papers.use_case: Designing and implementing task planning components for autonomous robotic systems or other complex agentic applications. This research helps AI engineers understand when to integrate LLMs for planning, how to structure prompts for effective plan generation, and when classical symbolic methods might still be superior or necessary for robust, verifiable performance.
